variables.name: I16810
variables.description: U1. Unchanging facial expression. The patient's face appears wooden--changes less than expected as emotional content of discourse changes. (Participants with European ancestry). DIGS2

variables.name: I16820
variables.description: U2. Decreased spontaneous movements. The patient shows few or no spontaneous movements, does not shift position, move extremities, etc. (Participants with European ancestry). DIGS2

variables.name: I16830
variables.description: U3. Paucity of expressive gestures. The patient does not use hand gestures or body position as an aid in expressing his ideas. (Participants with European ancestry). DIGS2

variables.name: I16840
variables.description: U4. Poor eye contact. The patient avoids eye contact or stares through interviewer even when speaking. (Participants with European ancestry). DIGS2

variables.name: I16850
variables.description: U5. Affective nonresponsivity. The patient fails to laugh or smile when prompted. (Participants with European ancestry). DIGS2

variables.name: I16860
variables.description: U6. Inappropriate affect. The patient's affect is inappropriate or incongruous, not simply flat or blunted. (Participants with European ancestry). DIGS2

variables.name: I16870
variables.description: U7. Lack of vocali inflections. The patient fails to show normal vocal emphasis patterns, is often monotonic. (Participants with European ancestry). DIGS2

variables.name: I16880
variables.description: U8. Global rating of affective flattening. This rating should focus on overall severity of symptoms, especially unresponsiveness, inappropriateness and an overall decrease in emotional intensity. (Participants with European ancestry). DIGS2

variables.name: I16890
variables.description: U9. Poverty of speech. The patient's replies to questions are restricted in amount, tend to be brief, concrete, unelaborated. (Participants with European ancestry). DIGS2

variables.name: I1690
variables.description: D2d. Have you ever been bothered by problems with pains in your arms or legs (other than in the joints)? (Participants with European ancestry). DIGS2

variables.name: I16900
variables.description: U10. Poverty of content of speech. The patient's replies are adequate in amount but tend to be vague, over concrete or over generalized, and convey little in information. (Participants with European ancestry). DIGS2

variables.name: I16910
variables.description: U11. Blocking. The patient indicates, either spontaneously or with prompting, that histrain of thought was interrupted. (Participants with European ancestry). DIGS2

variables.name: I16920
variables.description: U12. Increased latency of response. The patient takes a long time to reply to questions, prompting indicates the patient is aware of the question. (Participants with European ancestry). DIGS2

variables.name: I16930
variables.description: U13. Global rating of alogia. The core features of alogia are poverty of speech and poverty of content. (Participants with European ancestry). DIGS2

variables.name: I16940

variables.name: I16960

variables.name: I17623

variables.name: I17624
variables.description: W10. The following items should be rated after the interview. Rate Questions 1 - 27 from observation during the interview. INTERVIEWER: Rate goal-directedness of speech/thought. Did the subject stick to the subject of the questions, and answer them in a direct, logical manner? Or did the subject digress from the subject under discussion? If so, how often and how far did the subject digress from the theme being discussed? Include here circumstantiality, that is, digressions that eventually make it back to the subject under discussion, and vagueness, and inability to follow the subject's thinking pattern clearly. (Participants with European ancestry). DIGS2

variables.name: I17625
variables.description: W11. The following items should be rated after the interview. Rate Questions 1 - 27 from observation during the interview. INTERVIEWER: Rate organization of associations. Did the subject's associations during the interview make sense? Could you follow the subject's line of reasoning? With many individuals, even though they are digressive, it is easy to follow their lines of digression. With others, this is much more difficult. Take into account educational level, accents, articulation difficulties, etc. (Participants with European ancestry). DIGS2

variables.name: I17626

variables.name: A1A
variables.description: Have you ever had a time in your life when you felt sad, blue, or depressed for two weeks or more in a row? (Participants with European ancestry)

variables.name: A1B
variables.description: Have you ever had a time in your life lasting two weeks or more when you lost interest in most things like hobbies, work, or activities that usually give you pleasure? (Participants with European ancestry)

variables.name: A1C
variables.description: How much of the day did these feelings usually last? (Participants with European ancestry)

variables.name: A1D
variables.description: Please think of the two-week period in your life when your feelings of depression or loss of interest were worst: did you feel this way? ( every day, almost every day, less often).  (Participants with European ancestry)

variables.name: A2
variables.description: Did you feel more tired out or low on energy than is usual for you? (Participants with European ancestry)

variables.name: A3
variables.description: Did you gain or lose weight without trying, or did you stay about the same weight? (Participants with European ancestry)

variables.name: A3AA
variables.description: About how much weight did you gain? (Participants with European ancestry)

variables.name: A3AB
variables.description: About how much weight did you lose? (Participants with European ancestry)

variables.name: A3ACGAIN
variables.description: (Gained): about how much weight did you gain and lose? (Participants with European ancestry)

variables.name: A3ACLOST
variables.description: (Lost): about how much weight did you gain and lose? (Participants with European ancestry)

variables.name: A4
variables.description: Did you have more trouble falling asleep than you usually do? (Participants with European ancestry)

variables.name: A4A
variables.description: Did you have more trouble falling asleep than you usually do? If yes: how often did that happen? (Participants with European ancestry)

variables.name: A5
variables.description: Did you have a lot more trouble concentrating than usual? (Participants with European ancestry)

variables.name: A6
variables.description: People sometimes feel down on themselves, no good, or worthless. Did you feel this way? (Participants with European ancestry)

variables.name: A7
variables.description: Did you think a lot about death - either your own, someone else's, or death in general? (Participants with European ancestry)

variables.name: A1A
variables.description: Have you ever had a time in your life when you felt sad, blue, or depressed for two weeks or more in a row? (African American participants)

variables.name: A1B
variables.description: Have you ever had a time in your life lasting two weeks or more when you lost interest in most things like hobbies, work, or activities that usually give you pleasure? (African American participants)

variables.name: A1C
variables.description: How much of the day did these feelings usually last? (African American participants)

variables.name: A1D
variables.description: Did you feel this way: please think of two-week period in your life when your feelings of depression or loss of interest were worst (every day, almost every day, less often). (African American participants)

variables.name: A2
variables.description: Did you feel more tired out or low on energy than is usual for you? (African American participants)

variables.name: A3
variables.description: Did you gain or lose weight without trying, or did you stay about the same weight? (African American participants)

variables.name: A3AA
variables.description: About how much weight did you gain? (African American participants)

variables.name: A3AB
variables.description: About how much weight did you lose? (African American participants)

variables.name: A3ACGAIN
variables.description: (Gained): about how much weight did you gain and lose? (African American participants)

variables.name: A3ACLOST
variables.description: (Lost): about how much weight did you gain and lose? (African American participants)

variables.name: A4
variables.description: Did you have more trouble falling asleep than you usually do? (African American participants)

variables.name: A4A
variables.description: How often did that happen? (African American participants)

variables.name: A5
variables.description: Did you have a lot more trouble concentrating than usual? (African American participants)

variables.name: A6
variables.description: People sometimes feel down on themselves, no good, or worthless. Did you feel this way? (African American participants)

variables.name: A7
variables.description: Did you think a lot about death - either your own, someone else's, or death in general? (African American participants)

variables.name: A8
variables.description: About how many weeks altogether did you feel this way?  Count the weeks before, during and after the worst two weeks.  The total period of depression/loss of interest was. (African American participants)

variables.name: A8A
variables.description: How many periods like this did you have in your life, lasting two or more weeks? (African American participants)

variables.name: A8B
variables.description: About how old were you the FIRST time you had a period of two weeks like this?  (Whether or not you received any help for it?) Years of age when you first felt this way. (African American participants)

variables.name: A8C
variables.description: About how old were you the LAST time you had a period of two weeks like this?  (Whether or not you received any help for it?) Years of age when the most recent episode happened. (African American participants)

variables.name: A8D
variables.description: Did you ever tell a professional about these problems (medical doctor, psychologist, social worker, counselor, nurse, clergy, or other helping professional)? (African American participants)

variables.name: A8E
variables.description: Did you take medication or use drugs or alcohol more than once for these problems? (African American participants)

variables.name: A8F
variables.description: How much did these problems (feeling depression or loss of interest) interfere with your life or activities? (A lot, some, a little, not at all). (African American participants)

variables.description: Did you ever tell a professional about these problems (medical doctor, psychologist, social worker, counselor, nurse, clergy, or other helping professional)? (African American participants)

variables.name: B2YEARS

variables.name: G1
variables.description: The next questions are about how frequently you drink alcoholic beverages. By a "drink" we mean either: a bottle of beer, a wine cooler, a glass of wine, a shot of liquor,  or a mixed drink.  With these definitions in mind, what is the largest number of drinks you had in any single day ever? (African American participants)

variables.name: G2
variables.description: Was there ever a time in your life when your drinking or being hung over interfered with: your work at school, or your work at a job, or your home life or housework? (African American participants)

variables.name: G2A
variables.description: How often did drinking interfere with school, job or home life? (African American participants)

variables.name: G3
variables.description: Were you ever under the influence of alcohol in a situation where you could get hurt - like when driving a car or boat, using knives or guns or machinery, or anything else? (African American participants)

variables.name: G4
variables.description: Did you have any emotional or psychological problems from using alcohol - such as feeling uninterested in things, feeling depressed, suspicious of people, paranoid, or having strange ideas? (African American participants)

variables.name: G5
variables.description: Did you have such a strong desire or urge to drink that you could not keep from drinking? (African American participants)

variables.name: G6
variables.description: During that year, did you have a period of a month or more when you spent a great deal of time drinking or getting over the effects of alcohol? (African American participants)

variables.name: G7
variables.description: Did you ever have more to drink than you intended to, or did you drink much longer than you intended to? (African American participants)

variables.name: G7A
variables.description: During that year, how often did you drink more or longer than you intended to? (African American participants)

variables.name: G8
variables.description: During that year, was there ever a time when you had to drink much more than you used to drink, to get the same effect you wanted? (African American participants)

variables.name: H1_1
variables.description: The next questions are about your use of drugs on your own.  By "on your own" we mean either without a doctor's prescription, in larger amounts than prescribed, or for longer than prescribed.  With this definition in mind, did you ever use any of the following drugs on your own? Sedatives, including either barbiturates or sleeping pills? (Seconal, Halcion, Methaqualone, etc.). (African American participants)

variables.name: H1_2
variables.description: The next questions are about your use of drugs on your own.  By "on your own" we mean either without a doctor's prescription, in larger amounts than prescribed, or for longer than prescribed.  With this definition in mind, did you ever use any of the following drugs on your own? Tranquilizers or "nerve pills"? (Librium, Valium, Ativan, Meprobamate, Xanax, etc.). (African American participants)

variables.name: H1_3
variables.description: The next questions are about your use of drugs on your own.  By "on your own" we mean either without a doctor's prescription, in larger amounts than prescribed, or for longer than prescribed.  With this definition in mind, did you ever use any of the following drugs on your own? Amphetamines or other stimulants? (Methamphetamine, Preludin, Dexedrine, Ritalin, "Speed"). (African American participants)

variables.name: H1_4
variables.description: The next questions are about your use of drugs on your own.  By "on your own" we mean either without a doctor's prescription, in larger amounts than prescribed, or for longer than prescribed.  With this definition in mind, did you ever use any of the following drugs on your own? Analgesics or other prescription painkillers on your own? (This does NOT include normal use of aspirin, Tylenol without codeine, Advil, etc., but does include use of Tylenol with codeine and other prescription painkillers like Demerol, Darvon, Percodan, Codeine, Morphine, and Methadone). (African American participants)

variables.name: H1_5
variables.description: The next questions are about your use of drugs on your own.  By "on your own" we mean either without a doctor's prescription, in larger amounts than prescribed, or for longer than prescribed.  With this definition in mind, did you ever use any of the following drugs on your own? Inhalants that you sniff or breathe to get high or to feel good? (Amylnitrate, Freon, Nitrous Oxide, "Whippets", gasoline, spray paint, etc.). (African American participants)

variables.name: H1_6
variables.description: The next questions are about your use of drugs on your own. With this definition in mind, did you ever use any of the following drugs on your own? Marijuana or hashish? (African American participants)

(African American participants)

variables.name: H3
variables.description: Did your use of any of these substances ever interfere with: your work at school, or your work at a job, or your home life or housework? (African American participants)

variables.name: H3A
variables.description: How often did using any substances interfere with school, job or home life? (African American participants)

variables.name: H4
variables.description: Were you ever under the influence of any of these substances in a situation where you could get hurt - like when driving a car or boat, using knives or guns or machinery, or anything else? (African American participants)

variables.name: H5
variables.description: Did you have any emotional or psychological problems from using any of these substances - such as feeling uninterested in things, feeling depressed, suspicious of people, paranoid, or having strange ideas? (African American participants)

variables.name: H6
variables.description: Did you have such a strong desire or urge to use any of these substances that you could not keep from using it? (African American participants)

variables.name: H7
variables.description: Did you have a period of a month or more when you spent a great deal of time using any of these substances or getting over their effects? (African American participants)

variables.name: H8
variables.description: Did you ever use any of these substances in much larger amounts, or for a longer period of time, than you intended to? (African American participants)

variables.name: H8A
variables.description: During that year, how often did you use any of these substances more or longer than you intended to? (African American participants)

variables.name: H9
variables.description: Was there ever a time when you had to use more of any of these substances than you used to, to get the same effect you wanted? (African American participants)

variables.name: I1
variables.description: Have you ever been bothered by having certain unpleasant thoughts of your own that kept entering your mind against your wishes?  An example would be if you kept having the idea that your hands are dirty or have germs on them. (African American participants)

variables.name: I10
variables.description: Did you have a period when you had to say certain words over and over, either aloud or to yourself? (African American participants)

variables.name: I13
variables.description: Did you think that these actions were unnecessary or that you overdid it? (African American participants)

variables.name: I14
variables.description: Did you tell a doctor about having to do these things? (African American participants)

variables.name: I15
variables.description: Did having to do these things interfere with your life or work, or cause you difficulty with your relatives or friends, or upset you a great deal? (African American participants)

variables.name: I2
variables.description: Another example of an unpleasant thought would if you kept having the idea that you might harm someone, even though you really didn't want to.  Or you might have had thoughts you were ashamed of, but couldn't keep out of your mind.  Have you ever had any unpleasant and persistent thoughts like that? (African American participants)

variables.name: I3
variables.description: Did some of these thoughts seem to you to be unreasonable? (African American participants)

variables.name: I4
variables.description: Did these thoughts keep coming back again and again into your mind no matter how hard you tried to resist, ignore, or get rid of them? (African American participants)

variables.name: I5
variables.description: Did you ever tell a doctor about these thoughts? (African American participants)

variables.name: I6
variables.description: Did thinking about these ideas interfere with your life or work, or cause you difficulty with your relatives or friends, or upset you a great deal? (African American participants)

variables.name: I7
variables.description: Some people have the unpleasant feeling that they have to do something over and over again even though they know it is foolish, but they can't resist doing it - things like washing their hands again and again or going back several times to be sure they've locked a door or turned off the stove.  Have you ever had to do something like that over and over? (African American participants)

variables.name: I8
variables.description: Was there a time when you felt you had to do something in a certain order, like getting dressed perhaps, and had to start all over again if you did it in the wrong order? (African American participants)

variables.name: I9
variables.description: Has there been a period of several weeks when you felt you had to count something, like the squares in a tile floor, and couldn't resist doing it even when you tried to? (African American participants)
